conditions: Confusion arises when students assess cool-climate wines without distinguishing between wines from under-ripe fruit (green, high malic acid, harsh) and fully ripe cool-climate fruit (citrus, stone fruit, floral, balanced high acidity). The confusion is reinforced when students encounter green-herbal wines and incorrectly attribute the character to region rather than to harvest timing.
explanation: Cool climate and under-ripeness produce overlapping aromatic signals (particularly methoxypyrazine-driven herbaceous notes) but are not the same thing. A fully ripe cool-climate wine from Chablis or Mosel does not taste green or unripe — it shows citrus, mineral, and floral characters. The confusion leads to systematic misassessment in SAT: a student who conflates cool climate with under-ripeness will incorrectly describe ripe Chablis as showing under-ripe characters, or conversely, will describe a poorly made wine's green faults as 'cool climate style'.
